title: Scout Net
desc: Queen's Gate defeats stealth with a web of fine lines, powder trays, and creatures obsessive enough to maintain both.

steps.text:
The official explanation for why stealth fails at Queen's Gate is simple: the Danians have prepared for it. The practical explanation is more interesting. Fine tension cords run across the lesser approaches, powder trays sit under the side arches to record movement, and scout watchers know exactly how both should look when nothing is there. Today one whole section of the anti-stealth net has been disturbed by weather and routine traffic, which means it needs to be made fussy again before evening patrols come in.

The scout captain hands you a spool of line and a shallow tray of pale tracking dust. "Retension the cord grid if you want to work above the steps," she says. "Or refill and level the powder trays if you would rather work low and leave footprints for demonstration only."

options.label: Retension the anti-stealth cord grid across the side approach
Refill and level the tracking trays under the arch

steps.text:
The cord grid is finer than it first appears, enough to be ignored by creatures who think seeing is the only relevant sense and just heavy enough to tremble the moment anything crosses where it should not. Three lines have sagged, and one upper anchor has slipped enough to throw the whole pattern out of true. You retie the anchor, draw the lines back into their marked spacing, and test each run with the scout captain tapping them in sequence while you watch the shiver travel across the frame.

Once corrected, the grid becomes almost invisible again, which is precisely its virtue. The captain studies the restored pattern from three angles, then nods. "Good. If anything unseen passes now, it will have to explain itself to the cords first." At Queen's Gate, that is a respectable level of suspicion.

options.label: Coil the spare line and leave the grid waiting quietly

steps.text:
The tracking trays are shallow stone pans set flush with the side passages, filled with fine pale dust that records the slightest crossing long before the recognition stones or lane clerks would know to object. Today one tray has clumped from damp and another is so scuffed by routine inspection that it would not show a clean print if a whole patrol marched through it. You empty both, dry the pans, and sift fresh dust in thin even layers until each tray lies flat and untouched as a promise.

The captain tests one with the edge of a claw, then smooths it again with a brush until the surface returns to perfect stillness. It is almost beautiful in its own severe way. "There," she says. "Now stealth may continue believing in itself. The gate does not."

options.label: Set the brush aside and leave the trays reading cleanly
